input_path: data\papers\Ramdhan et al. - 2025 - Engineering Automotive Digital Twins on Standardized Architectures A Case Study.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: 1/10‑scale autonomous vehicle (teleoperated) retrofitted with advanced sensor and control technology, comprising lower and upper chassis layers and autonomy elements, operating in the vehicle dynamics domain for adaptive cruise control; chosen for digital twinning due to its manageable size, embedded sensor suite enabling real‑time state monitoring, and suitability for off‑loading computationally intensive tasks.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin offers three primary services: (1) Data Monitoring – a UI that displays real‑time vehicle state data, projected driving path, and detected object tracks, and allows data saving and report generation; (2) Computation Offloading – offloading the ACC decision‑making (lead‑vehicle detection and desired‑speed calculation) to cloud servers, reducing on‑board ECU load, and returning the desired speed for actuation; (3) Remote Management – enabling operators to remotely enable/disable ACC, set desired speed, and issue emergency braking commands via a UI, backed by low end‑to‑end latency communication to satisfy safety‑critical requirements.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: Implementation relies on NVIDIA JetPack 5.0 SDK for hardware acceleration and ROS 2 for middleware communication. Application modules are coded in Python 3.8 and C++, with additional control logic designed in MATLAB/Simulink.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: Time synchronization between the Physical Twin Entity and Digital Twin Entity is defined to achieve a 100 ms accuracy, meeting the hardware limitations while supporting safety‑critical vehicular applications. Each ROS2 package communicates timestamps using the ROS2 clock mechanism, and the system architecture ensures that control‑loop latency between sensor data acquisition, processing, and actuation stays within the 100 ms bound. No finer granular synchronisation or higher frequency coordination is specified.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The system implements a single Digital Twin instance composed of three distinct entities—User, Digital Twin, and Physical Twin—each instantiated on separate hardware nodes. The architecture is distributed but centrally coordinated via the Digital Twin Entity’s data‑management infrastructure. The architecture does not describe multiple twin instances or a hierarchic cluster of twins; thus multiplicity is effectively one, with the Digital Twin Entity orchestrating overall control.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The design follows the ISO 23247 reference architecture, mapping each Software Component (SW) to a Functional Entity (FE) and deploying each FE as an individual ROS2 package containing ROS2 nodes. The system is partitioned into three entities: the User Entity hosting the User Interface FE (split into 3D visualization and control panel), the Digital Twin Entity handling data management and control mechanisms, and the Physical Twin Entity encapsulating device communication, data preprocessing, actuation, and automotive functionality such as point‑cloud clustering and tracking. Resource management is handled through ROS2 middleware, which facilitates communication across distributed nodes, while a central entity aggregates data transformation and high‑level control, thereby coordinating the distributed twins.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The Digital Twin architecture follows ISO 23247 Part 2, with an entity‑based reference model comprising a Data Collection & Device Control Entity, the Digital Twin Entity, and a User Entity. The Digital Twin Entity holds computational representations of the Observable Manufacturing Element (OME): geometric models (CAD meshes of equipment or vehicle chassis), behavioral dynamics (kinematic and control equations for actuators and sensor platforms), and analytical models (performance metrics such as acceleration curves or energy consumption). Data flows from the OME through the Data Collection & Device Control Entity via sensor streams (e.g., LiDAR, RGB camera, depth camera) and device‑control interfaces. A dedicated Data Storage FE persists raw and processed data into a SQL database for auditability and further processing. Model relationships are managed by the Digital Twin FE; for example, the ACC (Adaptive Cruise Control) model receives speed/position data, generates acceleration set‑points, and forwards actuation commands to the Device Control FE. The overall constellation is engineered so that each FE encapsulates a single concern (data ingestion, model execution, user visualisation) and exposes well‑defined interfaces (OPC UA, MQTT, REST) for interoperability.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: Sensing is performed by a Hokuyo UST‑10LX LiDAR that scans the environment for object detection, and an Intel DeepSense D435i Depth Camera that captures vehicle state and depth information. The data streams from the LiDAR, depth camera, and additional auxiliary sensors are fused in real time to provide a comprehensive situational awareness of the vehicle and surroundings. Sampling frequencies, transmission rates, accuracy, and precision values are not specified in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Sensor data from external LiDAR, vehicle state (speed, position, and motion) sensors, and actuator feedback are captured by the Physical Twin Entity and transmitted to the Digital Twin via a dedicated Data Translation component. The Data Translation serialises the raw Point Cloud and vehicle state messages, aligns message types, performs unit conversions, and forwards the processed streams to the ROS2-based services inside the Digital Twin. These streams are then consumed by the Data Monitoring service for real‑time 3‑D visualization. Communication from the physical side to the digital side occurs over a Wi‑Fi network using UDP transport, while intra‑digital communication is handled by a ROS2 network.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: Control commands originating from the User Interface FE (e.g., acceleration, brake, steering requests) are routed through the Digital Twin’s Actuation FE components (Motor Actuation FE and Steering Servo Actuation FE) using the ROS2 network. Prior to transmission, the Data Translation component re‑serialises the commands, ensuring consistent message format and units. The actuation commands are then sent to the physical actuators over the Wi‑Fi network with UDP transport. Feedback of updated vehicle state information returned by the Vehicle State FE provides confirmation of command execution, allowing the Digital Twin to reconcile command status with the real‑world response.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: The Digital Twin Service is built on a hybrid network architecture. Software components within the same physical host communicate via ROS 2 over an internal bus, while inter‑entity communication (between the vehicle and the virtual twin) takes place over a Wi-Fi network using UDP. The system must honour a maximum network latency of 100 ms, as specified by Requirement R7. Bandwidth and reliability requirements beyond this latency constraint are not explicitly quantified. No explicit encryption or authentication protocols are specified for the network traffic in the provided documents.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The Digital Twin system aligns with the ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manufacturing. The reference architecture (Part 2 of the standard) specifies an entity‑based architecture comprising: Observable Manufacturing Element (OME), Data Collection and Device Control Entity, Digital Twin Entity, and User Entity, each composed of Functional Entities (FEs). The architecture supports digital representation of physical manufacturing elements (Part 3) and information exchange procedures (Part 4). Compliance is demonstrated by referencing the standard parts and by implementing data collection and control via OPC Unified Architecture, an interoperability protocol adopted by the standard.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
_A. Context_


DT technology enables an array of benefits for ACC through
off-loading any critical or non-critical computation for which
resource-constrained electronic control units (ECU) are typically not sufficient. To implement the ACC-DT, we develop
the following three services through a DT.
_1) Data Monitoring:_ Monitoring ACC-relevant data flowing through a vehicle is crucial for tracking system performance. The DT presented in this case will contain a user
interface (UI) allowing a user to view, save, and generate
reports of vehicle state data. Additionally, the UI will display
real-time vehicle state data, the projected driving path, and
detected object tracks in the vehicle’s proximity.
_2) Computation Offloading:_ A DT can alleviate the computational burden placed on resource-constrained on-board
hardware by allocating compute to better-suited equipment,
e.g., in the cloud. In this case study, we demonstrate this
functionality by offloading only the ACC system from the
vehicle to the DT. The ACC system determines the lead
vehicle, then computes a desired speed based on the distance
to the lead vehicle, finally sending the desired speed back to
the vehicle where the actuation is carried out.


(a) Base hardware: teleoperated vehicle retrofitted with advanced
sensor and control technology



(b) Main hardware layers of the physical twin: lower and upper level
chassis, and the autonomy elements



Fig. 1: The physical twin: a 1/10 [th] -scale autonomous vehicle



_3) Remote Management:_ The ability to remotely manage
ADAS systems enables the development, testing, and operation
of these systems from a different geographical location than
the physical twin. In addition to displaying real-time state data,
the UI will also enable the management and operation of ACC
remotely. This includes enabling/disabling ACC, setting the
desired speed, and sending an emergency braking command.
To implement these services, the DT must support low
end-to-end latency communication with the vehicle, contain
data collection and reporting capabilities, and more—all while
respecting the safety-critical nature of the system. For convenience and following community best practices, we refer
to the collection of the DT Entity, physical and middleware
components, and the UI as the digital twin system (DTS).
The users of our system can be grouped into two classes:
operators and developers. Operators use the DT to monitor
and remotely manage ACC, and developers use the DT to
troubleshoot during development using the data collection and
monitoring capabilities. In the _Liberty to act vs. Ability to act_
taxonomy from David and Bork [30], this DTS falls under the
Fully Autonomous Digital Twin, with the Human-in-the-Loop.


_B. Physical Twin_

It also correctly identifies the suitability for digital twinning regarding off-loading computation and real-time monitoring. It provides specific details such as the UI capabilities for saving/reports, the offloading of lead-vehicle detection and speed calculation, and the specific remote commands (enable/disable, set speed, emergency brake). Quotes: '1) Data Monitoring: Monitoring ACC-relevant data flowing through a vehicle... UI allowing a user to view, save, and generate reports', '2) Computation Offloading: ...offloading only the ACC system from the vehicle to the DT... ACC system determines the lead vehicle, then computes a desired speed', '3) Remote Management: ...enable/disabling ACC, setting the desired speed, and sending an emergency braking command'.

The extraction correctly lists the specific development tools and frameworks used: NVIDIA JetPack 5.0 SDK, ROS 2 middleware, and the programming languages Python 3.8 and C++, as well as MATLAB/Simulink. These details are explicitly found in Section III.B: 'We use NVIDIA Jetpack 5.0 as the Software Development Kit (SDK)... and Robot Operating System 2 (ROS2)... as the middleware... Application modules are implemented in Python 3.8... C++... and MATLAB/Simulink'.

block_2_docs_retrieved:
coordination between devices. To enable computation
offloading, devices must share the same notion of time.
Many standards mandate stricter clock synchronization
than 100 ms for safety-critical vehicular applications. We
require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations.


_B. Architecture_


We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design
phase. First, we identify key software (SW) components
(Tab. I) and their relationships to each other, and to the sensors
and actuators of the physical twin (Fig. 2).
Next, we map the SW components onto FEs and implement
them. To enable their execution on the vehicle, we implement
each FE as an individual ROS2 package. Each package contains one or more ROS2 nodes implementing the functionality
provided by the FE. This mapping helps organize the SW
components by the entity-based structure of ISO 23247.
User-facing components are organized into the _User Entity_,
within which the singular _User Interface FE_ is instantiated
in the _3D Visualization_ and _Control Panel_ components. SW
components that implement data management and control
mechanisms are organized into the _Digital Twin Entity_ . Data
pre-processing, actuation, and parts of the device communication functionality are organized into the _Physical Twin_
_Entity_ . The existence of this entity marks a departure from
the ISO 23247 RA. Most similar to the Physical Twin Entity
in the standard is the OME, which hosts resource-specific
functionality. By contrast, in our design, the Physical Twin
Entity reflects the inherent resources of the twinned system (e.g., compute, sensors, actuators) and can host general
automotive functionality, such as _Point Cloud Clustering &_
_Tracking_ . Considering that each entity has been deployed on
a different hardware component, such deviations from the
standard are substantial as they may impact the ownership of
the functionality and the engineering process of the DTS.
Not every ISO 23247 FE was required for our DTS. At the
same time, the _User Interface FE_ has been split into two subcomponents: visualization and control panel. Finally, one SW
component, _Data Storage_ did not have a naturally aligned FE.


**Physical Twin Entity*** **Digital Twin Entity** **User Entity**

In addition, a demonstration of the DT is publicly available. [2]


II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.
For example, one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture

It also claims the DT holds 'CAD meshes of equipment,' 'kinematic and control equations,' and 'acceleration curves or energy consumption' models, which are not mentioned in the specific case study implementation (only general background references to 3D CAD). Due to the inclusion of multiple unsupported technical claims, the score is low.

The document describes physical actuators like the 'Vedder Electronic Speed Controller' and 'Steering Servo,' and control mechanisms such as the 'Actuation FE' which 'converts desired speed into motor voltage commands.' It also outlines remote control capabilities, including emergency braking and speed adjustment via the 'Control Panel.' While detailed, it could include more specifics on actuation ranges or safety constraints.

The extraction is highly specific and well-supported by the text. It correctly identifies the 'Hokuyo UST-10LX LiDAR' and 'Intel Deepsense D435i Depth Camera' listed as 'onboard sensors' in Section III.B. It accurately describes the data fusion process, supported by Section II.B: 'Streams of data from LiDAR, cameras, and other sensors are fused in real time'. It also correctly notes the absence of specific technical metrics like sampling frequencies and accuracy in the provided text.

The extraction states 'Not in Document'. The provided text cuts off before such sections would typically appear.

Implementation of Services_ Below, we describe each DTS service as a composition of multiple SW components, summarized in Tab. II. All of the DTS services rely on the Data Translation component, which corresponds to the ISO 23247 Data Translation FE. This component serializes data, aligns message types, and performs unit conversions for every data stream in the system. Communication between SW components takes place within a ROS2 network, while inter-entity communication occurs over a Wi-Fi network using UDP in the transport layer. _1) Data Monitoring:_ To enable data monitoring, ACCrelevant data such as vehicle state information, object tracks, and projected driving path are displayed in real-time on the 3D Visualization, as shown in Fig. 3a. II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
